source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure network access to IBM Cloud Databases service is set to be exposed on “Private end points only”

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure network access to IBM Cloud Databases service is set to be exposed on “Private end points only”
input_row.description: All Cloud Databases deployments offer integration with IBM Cloud Service Endpoints. It gives you the ability to enable connections to your deployments from the public internet and over the IBM Cloud Private network. Service Endpoints are available in all IBM Cloud Multi-Zone Regions and some Single- Zone Regions. If your deployments are in Osl01, you aren't able to use private endpoints. Deployments in all other regions are able to use Service Endpoints. Public endpoints provide a connection to your deployment on the public network. At provision time, a public endpoint is the default option for all deployments. Your environment needs to have internet access to connect to a deployment. A deployment with a service endpoint on the private network gets an endpoint that is not accessible from the public internet. All traffic is routed to hardware dedicated to Cloud Databases deployments and remains on the IBM Cloud Private network. All traffic to and from this endpoint is free and unmetered as long as the traffic remains in IBM Cloud. Once your environment has access to the IBM Cloud Private network, an internet connection is not required to connect to your deployment.
input_row.rationale: The use of public endpoints exposes data that is contained in the database to unnecessary risk from the outside internet. Impact: Some applications are not run in the IBM Cloud. Therefore, they cannot rely on the IBM Cloud network backbone to maintain communication from application to the database over the private network. In this case, the customer may choose to enable "Public" endpoints which allow access to the database from the internet. Customers that are required to do should IP Whitelist the database before enabling "Public" endpoints.
input_row.audit: From the Console: 1. Log on to your IBM Cloud account 2. Go to the Menu icon > Resource List to access your list of account resources 3. Click the Database service you are interested in to open the service dashboard. 4. Ensure you are on the Manage page in the left pane. 5. Click the Settings tab in the middle of the page and scroll down to Service endpoints 6. Review the toggle that displays the status of Private endpoint and Public endpoint enablement. Ensure, Public is toggled off and Private is toggled on . From the API: 1. Access to the API uses token authentication, by using the header Authorization: Bearer . The token must be IAM-issued. You can send in an IAM API key directly as the token or use the API key to generate an IAM Bearer Token. 2. Insert the region your deployment is in and the CRN of your deployment as indicated by {} curl -X GET https://api.{region}.databases.cloud.ibm.com/v4/ibm/deployments/{id} -H 'Authorization: Bearer <>' \ This will return something like: { "deployment": { "id": "crn", "name": "crn", "type": "database", "platform_options": { "key_protect_key_id": "" }, "version": "x.y", "admin_username": "admin", "enable_private_endpoints": true, "enable_public_endpoints": false } } Ensure that enable_private_endpoints is true and that enable_public_endpoints is false .
input_row.remediation: From the Console: 1. Log on to your IBM Cloud account 2. Go to the Menu icon > Resource List to access your list of account resources 3. Click the Database service you are interested in to open the service dashboard. 4. Ensure you are on the Manage page in the left pane. 5. Click the Settings tab in the middle of the page and scroll down to Service endpoints 6. Select Private endpoint toggle to be turned on and Public endpoint to be turned off . 7. Click Update Endpoints From the CLI: You can use the ibmcloud resource service-instance-update command in the CLI, specifying the endpoint with the --service-endpoints flag. This can be done online with no downtime ibmcloud resource service-instance-update <service-name> --service-endpoints <endpoint-type> Changing the type of endpoints available on your deployment does not cause any downtime from a database perspective. However, if you disable an endpoint that is being used by you or your applications, those connections are dropped. From the API: You can use the Resource Controller API, with a PATCH request to the /resource_instances/{id} endpoint. **Figure out more detail here, should be copy and paste for every service **
input_row.impact: Some applications are not run in the IBM Cloud. Therefore, they cannot rely on the IBM Cloud network backbone to maintain communication from application to the database over the private network. In this case, the customer may choose to enable "Public" endpoints which allow access to the database from the internet. Customers that are required to do should IP Whitelist the database before enabling "Public" endpoints.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The compliance control can be fully verified using IBM Cloud APIs and IBM Cloud CLI.
- The audit field provides clear instructions for using API calls to verify endpoint configurations, indicating automation feasibility.
- IBM Cloud provides API support for querying database deployment settings, specifically the `enable_private_endpoints` and `enable_public_endpoints` properties.
- The control fits the criteria for standard IBM Cloud service property checks, specifically regarding network endpoint configurations.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_cloud_databases_service_private_endpoint_only

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the IBM Cloud API to check the endpoint settings for each Cloud Databases deployment.
- Perform API GET requests to `https://api.{region}.databases.cloud.ibm.com/v4/ibm/deployments/{id}` with appropriate authentication headers using an IAM Bearer Token.
- Enumerate all database deployments for the given account and region.
- Validate the `enable_private_endpoints` is set to `true` and `enable_public_endpoints` is set to `false`.
- IBM Cloud CLI can also be used with the `ibmcloud resource service-instance-update` command to programmatically ensure endpoints are configured correctly after initial verification.
- Pass/Fail Criteria:
  - **Pass:** `enable_private_endpoints` is `true` and `enable_public_endpoints` is `false`.
  - **Fail:** Any deviation from the expected settings.
- Automation can enable regular scanning of the account to continuously enforce this configuration.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of IBM Cloud APIs and IBM Cloud CLI is appropriate for verifying the network access settings of IBM Cloud Databases. The APIs provide the necessary capabilities to query and validate endpoint configurations programmatically.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of API GET requests to the specified endpoint with IAM Bearer Token authentication is correct. The criteria for passing or failing the control are well-defined.
- The IBM Cloud CLI command mentioned (`ibmcloud resource service-instance-update`) is valid for updating service instance configurations.
- The program name follows a reasonable convention, but it could be more descriptive to enhance clarity.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved to better reflect the specific security check being performed. A suggestion could be `ibm_cloud_databases_network_private_endpoint_check`.
- Consider adding a step to log the results of the API checks for audit trail purposes.
- Ensure that the IAM Bearer Token is managed securely, possibly by integrating with IBM Cloud Secrets Manager for token storage.
- Verify if there are any additional regions or exceptions similar to Osl01 that might affect the applicability of private endpoints.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-founded with a clear understanding of IBM Cloud API capabilities and security best practices. The technical details align with IBM Cloud's documentation and capabilities.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name to `ibm_cloud_databases_network_private_endpoint_check` for improved clarity.
- Keep the approach as Automated, as it is correctly leveraging IBM Cloud's automation capabilities for compliance verification.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that IBM Cloud exposes the necessary database endpoint configuration properties (`enable_private_endpoints`, `enable_public_endpoints`) via its APIs and CLI, allowing for programmatic verification. The audit steps described in the control can be fully replicated using API calls, and there are no requirements for manual inspection or console-only settings. This control is a standard configuration check that is fully automatable using IBM Cloud's documented API endpoints.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
ibm_cloud_databases_service_private_endpoint_only

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- IBM Cloud Resource/Service: IBM Cloud Databases (all managed database services supporting private/public endpoints)
- API/CLI Method: Use the IBM Cloud Databases API (e.g., GET `https://api.{region}.databases.cloud.ibm.com/v4/ibm/deployments/{id}`) with IAM Bearer Token authentication; optionally, IBM Cloud CLI (`ibmcloud resource service-instance` and related commands)
- Properties to Check:
  - `enable_private_endpoints` (must be `true`)
  - `enable_public_endpoints` (must be `false`)
- Pass Criteria:  
  - For each database deployment, `enable_private_endpoints` is `true` AND `enable_public_endpoints` is `false`
- Fail Criteria:  
  - Any deployment where `enable_private_endpoints` is not `true` OR `enable_public_endpoints` is not `false`

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach. The only minor difference was on the program name; I selected the more direct and descriptive name that aligns with IBM Cloud naming conventions and the control's intent.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
